title: Task Queue
description: The you-agent-factory task queue is the backlog of submitted work waiting in work-type states until workstations consume it.
openingSummary: A task queue is the factory backlog of submitted work sitting in work-type states and places until enabled workstations can consume ready items.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A task queue is the you-agent-factory backlog of submitted work. After work enters the factory, each item becomes a token waiting in a work-type state and place. Those waiting places hold the work until a workstation whose inputs match can consume a ready item. The queue is that waiting backlog—not the submit step that enqueues work, and not the workstation that later runs it.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Long-running factories keep many work items alive after submit. The task queue is how those items stay persistent and ready for dispatch: workstations can take them in parallel or in order as matching inputs become available, instead of losing the backlog when a single agent session ends.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: You submit a work item into a running factory. It lands in an initial work-type state such as task:init, which is a queued place in the topology. A workstation whose inputs match that place becomes enabled, consumes the waiting item, and moves it forward. Until that consume happens, the item stays in the task queue.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: The task queue is the runtime backlog of waiting work, not a planner checklist, not the submit batch that enqueues work, not the token vocabulary itself, and not the workqueue-executor technique pattern.
